body: There is a work around: use the colors defined in your terminal (the base 16 colors).

Hello!

I'd love to help implement this. Building on the existing palette / palettes infrastructure, this could be done cleanly with backward compatibility.

A rough example would be how [bat does it](https://github.com/sharkdp/bat/pull/2896) — using [terminal-colorsaurus](https://github.com/bash-lsp/terminal-colorsaurus)vfor OSC 11 terminal background detection.

Config would look like:

```
   palette = "auto"
   palette_dark = "catppuccin-mocha"
   palette_light = "catppuccin-latte"

   [palettes.catppuccin-mocha]
   text = "#cdd6f4"
   surface = "#313244"

   [palettes.catppuccin-latte]
   text = "#4c4f69"
   surface = "#ccd0da"
```

If there is interest in support for this I could do the following (either non-AI assisted or non-vibe coded basic AI assistance, whichever is preferred by the community):

   1. Add palette_dark: Option<String> and palette_light: Option<String> to StarshipRootConfig in src/configs/starship_root.rs
   2. When palette is set to "auto", query the terminal color scheme via terminal-colorsaurus and select palette_dark or palette_light accordingly
   3. Cache the detection result for the lifetime of the prompt render (since starship runs on every prompt, we only need to detect once per invocation)
   4. Fall back to palette_dark if detection fails, or to the existing palette behavior if palette_dark/palette_light aren't configured
   5. Update get_palette() in src/config.rs to handle the resolution logic

This keeps full backward compatibility — existing palette = "my-palette" configs continue to work unchanged.

Thoughts?

Hi — I built a tiny detector that lets Starship adapt to dark/light themes: rh42-ic/term-color-detector.

How it works (short):
- Run the detector from your shell rc (bash/zsh/fish) at startup.
- It detects terminal theme (OSC 11) and sets STARSHIP_CONFIG to a pre-made starship-dark.toml / starship-light.toml.
- Lightweight: runs once at shell startup, negligible overhead so prompt latency is unchanged. The other CLIs no longer need to call OSC11 again.

```bash
# Detect terminal color scheme (default to 'light' on failure or timeout)
# We use a 200ms timeout to account for network latency in SSH
if THEME=$(term-color-det -s -t 200 2>/dev/null); then
    export TERMINAL_SCHEME="$THEME"
else
    export TERMINAL_SCHEME="light"
fi

# Example usage: Set theme for 'bat' and 'starship'
if [ "$TERMINAL_SCHEME" = "light" ]; then
    export BAT_THEME="GitHub"
    export STARSHIP_CONFIG="$HOME/.config/starship.light.toml"
else
    export BAT_THEME="Catppuccin-Mocha"
    export STARSHIP_CONFIG="$HOME/.config/starship.dark.toml"
fi
```

Repo:  [rh42-ic/term-color-detector](https://github.com/rh42-ic/term-color-detector)
